Question: A bulb emits light of wavelength 900 nm. The bulb is rated as 150 watt and 8% of the energy is emitted as light. The number of photons emitted by the bulb in one second is $x$ x 10<sup>18</sup>. The value of $x$ is (in nearest integer)
Solution:
Energy emitted $ = \frac{{8 \times 150}}{{100}} = 12{\text{ Joules}}$
$E = n\frac{{hc}}{\lambda } \Rightarrow n = \frac{{E.\lambda }}{{hc}}$
$n = \frac{{12 \times 900 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}}{{6.626 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 5.43 \times {10^{19}}$

Question: \[{A^{n + }}\] is oxisidised by acidified \[KMn{O_4}\] solution into \[A{O_3}^ - \]. If \[2.68\,{\text{millimoles }}{{\text{A}}^{n + }}\] requires \[32.16\,mL\] of a \[0.05\,M\] acidified solution for complete oxidation, find the value of \[n\].
Solution:
n-factor for conversion of \[{A^{n + }}\] to \[A{O_3}^ - = \left( {5 - n} \right)\]
Now, the number of equivalents of \[{A^{n + }} = KMn{O_4}\]
\[2.68 \times \left( {5 - n} \right) = 5 \times 0.05 \times 32.16\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {5 - n} \right) = \frac{{5 \times 0.05 \times 32.16}}{{2.68}} = 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow n = 2\]

Question: A compound microscope consists of an objective lens of focal length 1 cm and an eye piece of focal length 5 cm with a separation of 10 cm between them. The distance between an object and the objective lens, at which the strain on the eye is maximum, is 35/k cm The value of k is
Solution:
Maximum strain ⇒ Max. angular magnification
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \left| {{U_e}} \right| = \frac{{D{f_e}}}{{D + {f_e}}} = \frac{{25 \times 5}}{{30}} = \frac{{25}}{6} \\
  {V_0} = 10 - \frac{{25}}{6} = \frac{{35}}{6}{\text{ cm}} \\
\end{gathered} $
Lens formula for objective
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{35/6}} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{{ + 1}} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{6}{{35}} - 1 = \frac{1}{u} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{ - 29}}{{35}} = \frac{1}{u} \\
   \Rightarrow u = \frac{{ - 35}}{{29}} \\
   \Rightarrow \left| u \right| = + \frac{{35}}{{29}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A particle is revolving in a circle of radius 2 m with angular velocity $\omega  = {t^2} - 4t + 8$ rad/s. Find the time (in sec) when speed of the particle becomes 8 m/s.
Solution:
$v = 8\,m/s = R\omega $
$ \Rightarrow \omega = 4 = {t^2} - 4t + 8$
${t^2} - 4t + 4 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow t = 2$ sec

Question: The figure given below shows the unit cell of compound which is a mixed oxide of zirconium, calcium, and nickel. The formula of mixed oxide is ZraCabNicOd. The value of (a + b + c + d) is…… (each edge center has O2– ion)
Solution:
As shown in the given figure, single unit cell consists of three cubes.
Number of Ni4+ ions in each cube is 8.
∴ Number of Ni4+ ions = 8 × 3 (in three cubes) × 1/8 (per corner share) = 3 per unit cell.
Number of Ca2+ ions = 2 per unit cell
Number of Zr2+ ions =1 per unit cell
Number of O2− ions = 20 × 1/4 + 8  × 1/2 = 9 per unit cell
Therefore, formula of the compound can be Zr(1)2+Ca(2)2+Ni(3)4+O(9)2− as total positive charge (18) is equal to the total negative charge (18).
Thus, the value of a + b + c + d =15.

Question:
How many of the following statements are correct? [Ignore the exceptions, consider them as general statements]
(a) A small positive ion favours covalency.
(b) A large negative ion favours covalency.
(c) Large charges on either cation or anion, or on both ions, favour covalency.
(d) Polarization, and hence covalency, is favoured if the positive ion (having inner d electrons) does not have a noble gas configuration.
Solution:
All the given statements are true and explain Fajan’s rules.
\[ \Rightarrow \] The smaller the size of the cation and the larger the size of the anion, the greater the covalent character of an ionic bond.
\[ \Rightarrow \] The greater the charge on the cation or anion, the greater the covalent character of the ionic bond.
\[ \Rightarrow \] For cations of the same size and charge, the one, with electronic configuration (n-1)dnns0, typical of transition metals, is more polarising than the one with a noble gas configuration, ns2 np6, typical of alkali and alkaline earth metal cations.

Question: The system of equations $\lambda x + y + z = 1,x + \lambda y + z = \lambda $ and $x + y + \lambda z = {\lambda ^2}$ have no solution. Then the value of ${\lambda ^4}$ is
Solution:
$\Delta = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  \lambda &1&1 \\
  1&\lambda &1 \\
  1&1&\lambda
\end{array}} \right| = \left( {\lambda + 2} \right){\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)^2}$
${\Delta _x} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&1&1 \\
  \lambda &\lambda &1 \\
  {{\lambda ^2}}&1&\lambda
\end{array}} \right| = - \left( {\lambda + 1} \right){\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)^2},$
${\Delta _y} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  \lambda &1&1 \\
  1&\lambda &1 \\
  1&{{\lambda ^2}}&\lambda
\end{array}} \right| = - \lambda {\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)^2}$
${\Delta _z} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  \lambda &1&1 \\
  1&\lambda &\lambda \\
  1&1&{{\lambda ^2}}
\end{array}} \right| = {\left( {\lambda + 1} \right)^2}{\left( {\lambda - 1} \right)^2}$
If we have $\lambda = - 2$ then $\Delta = 0\,\,\& \,\,\,{\Delta _x},{\Delta _y},{\Delta _z} \ne 0$
Hence no solution for $\lambda = - 2$

Question:
The electric field in a region is given by $\vec E = \left( {\frac{3}{5}{E_0}\hat i + \frac{4}{5}{E_0}\hat j + \frac{4}{5}{E_0}\hat k} \right)\frac{N}{C}$. The ratio of flux of reported field through the rectangular surface of area 0.2 m2 (parallel to y – z plane) to that of the surface of area 0.3 m2 (parallel to x – z plane) is a : b, where a =__________. (Assume $a$ and $b$ are minimum positive integers)
[Here \[\widehat i,\,\widehat j\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\widehat k\] are unit vectors along x, y and z-axes respectively]
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

$\vec E = \left( {\frac{{3{E_0}}}{5}\hat i + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}j + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}\hat k} \right)\frac{N}{C}$
${A_1} = 0.2{m^2}$[parallel to $y - z$ plane]
$ = {\vec A_1} = 0.2{m^2}\hat i$
${A_2} = 0.3{m^2}$ [parallel to $x - z$ plane]
${\vec A_2} = 0.3{m^2}\hat j$
Now,
${\phi _a} = \left[ {\frac{{3{E_0}}}{5}\hat i + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}\hat j + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}\hat k} \right] \cdot \left[ {0.2\hat i} \right] = \frac{{3 \times 0.2}}{5}{E_0}$
${\phi _b} = \left[ {\frac{{3{E_0}}}{5}\hat i + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}\hat j + \frac{{4{E_0}}}{5}\hat k} \right] \cdot \left[ {0.3\hat i} \right] = \frac{{4 \times 0.3}}{5}{E_0}$
Now,
$\frac{{{\phi _a}}}{{{\phi _b}}} = \frac{{0.6}}{{1.2}} = \frac{1}{2} = \frac{a}{b}$
$ \Rightarrow a:b = 1:2$
$ \Rightarrow a = 1$.

Question:
Consider the following equations:
$2F{e^{2 + }} + {H_2}{O_2} \to x\,A + y\,B$(in basic medium)
$2MnO_4^ - + 6{H^ \oplus } + 5{H_2}{O_2} \to x'C + y'D + z'E$(in acidic medium).
The sum of the stoichiometric coefficients x, y, x’,y’ and z’ for products A, B, C, D and E, respectively, is________.

A circle passes through the point (3, 4) and cuts the circle \[{x^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}\] orthogonally. The locus of its centre is a straight line. If the distance of the straight line from the origin is 817, then find the value of \[{a^2} - 8140\].
Solution:
Let the equation of the circle be \[{x^2} + {y^2} + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0\]
Since, it passes through (3, 4), \[6g + 8f + c = - 25\].

As it cuts the circle \[{x^2} + {y^2} = {a^2}\] orthogonally

\[2g \times 0 + 2f \times 0 = c - {a^2}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,c = {a^2}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \]\[6g + 8f + {a^2} + 25 = 0\]

\[\therefore \]Locus of the centre \[\left( { - g,\,\, - f} \right)\] is \[6x + 8y - \left( {{a^2} + 25} \right) = 0\].

\[\therefore \]Distance of the line from the origin is \[817 = \frac{{{a^2} + 25}}{{\sqrt {36 + 64} }}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,{a^2} + 25 = 8170\]

\[ \Rightarrow \]\[{a^2} = 8145\]

Question: The entropy change for reversible transition of an element A into its allotropic form A* is –1 Joule/ K-mole at 2000 K and 15000 bar pressure. If the molar volume of A and A* are 20 cc and 19 cc per mole respectively, find the magnitude of ∆U for the transition process in (Joule/mole).
Solution:
\[\Delta {H_{transition}} = T\Delta S = - 2000{\text{ J}}\]
\[\Delta U = \Delta H - P\Delta V = - 2000 - 15000 \times {10^5}\left( {19 - 20} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}\]
\[\Delta U = - 2000 + 1500 = -500\;{\text{J/mole}}\]

Question: Variance of first 25 odd natural numbers is
Solution:
Variance of first n odd natural numbers
$ = \frac{{{1^2} + {3^2} + {5^2} + ...\,n\,\,{\text{terms}}}}{n} - {\left( {\frac{{1 + 3 + 5 + ...\,n\,{\text{terms}}}}{n}} \right)^2}$
$ = \frac{{\sum\limits_{n = 1}^n {{{\left( {2n - 1} \right)}^2}} }}{n} - {\left( {\frac{{\frac{n}{2}\left( {2 + \left( {n - 1} \right)2} \right)}}{n}} \right)^2}$
$ = \frac{{4\sum\limits_{n = 1}^n {{n^2}} + \sum\limits_{n = 1}^n 1 - 4\sum\limits_{n = 1}^n n }}{n} - {n^2}$
$ = \frac{{4\frac{{n\left( {n + 1} \right)\left( {2n + 1} \right)}}{6} + n - 4\frac{{n\left( {n + 1} \right)}}{2}}}{n} - {n^2}$
$ = \frac{{2\left( {n + 1} \right)\left( {2n + 1} \right)}}{3} + 1 - 2\left( {n + 1} \right) - {n^2}$
$ = \frac{{2\left( {n + 1} \right)\left( {2n + 1} \right)}}{3} - 2\left( {n + 1} \right) + \left( {1 - n} \right)\left( {1 + n} \right)$
$ = \left( {n + 1} \right)\left[ {\frac{{4n + 2}}{3} - 2 + 1 - n} \right]$
$ = \left( {n + 1} \right)\left( {\frac{{4n + 2}}{3} - 1 - n} \right)$
$ = \frac{{\left( {n + 1} \right)\left( {n - 1} \right)}}{3} = \frac{{{n^2} - 1}}{3}$
Hence, variance of first 25 odd natural numbers
$ = \frac{{{{25}^2} - 1}}{3} = \frac{{625 - 1}}{3} = \frac{{624}}{3} = 208$

Question: If function \[y = f\left( x \right)\] has a second order derivative \[f''\left( x \right) = 6\left( {x - 1} \right)\]. If its graph passes through the point \[\left( {2,1} \right)\] and at the point the tangent to the graph is \[y = 3x - 5\], then the function is \[{\left( {x + a} \right)^b}\]. The value of \[a + b\] is
Solution:
\[f''\left( x \right) = 6\left( {x - 1} \right)\]
\[f'\left( x \right) = 6\left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - x} \right) + c \Rightarrow f'\left( 2 \right) = c\]
Now, it is given that \[y = 3x - 5\] at \[x = 2 \Rightarrow y'\left( 2 \right) = 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3 = c\]
So,
\[f'\left( x \right) = 3{x^2} - 6x + 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x + d\]
So, it is given that \[y = 3x - 5\] at \[x = 2 \Rightarrow y\left( 2 \right) = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 = 8 - 12 + 6 + d\]
\[d = - 1\]
So,
\[f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 1\]
\[ = {x^3} - 3x\left( {x - 1} \right) - 1\]
\[ = {\left( {x - 1} \right)^3}\]

Question: At a certain height above the earth’s surface, the gravitational acceleration is 4% of its value at the surface of the earth. If the height is nR. Find n. (R is the radius of the earth)
Solution:
\[g' = g{\left[ {1 + \frac{h}{R}} \right]^{ - 2}} \to \] Because change in g is huge, so h is not very small as compared to Radius of Earth
\[\frac{4}{{100}}g = g{\left[ {1 + \frac{h}{R}} \right]^{ - 2}}\]
\[1 + \frac{h}{R} = \sqrt {25} = 5\]
h/R = 4
h = 4R ,n=4.

Question:
How many of the following have only four hybrid orbitals on the central atom of its molecule?
\[Xe{O_3},I_3^ - ,Xe{F_2},{H_2}O,PC{l_5},XeO_2F_2,SO_4^{ - 2},NH_4^ + \]
Solution:
Steric number gives the number of hybrid orbitals.
Steric number = \[\frac{1}{2}\left[ {V + M - C + A} \right]\]
V = Number of valence electrons on central atom
M = Number of monovalent groups attached to the central atom
C = Magnitude of charge on cation
A = Magnitude of charge on anion
MoleculeSteric Number
XeO3\[\frac{{8 + 0 - 0 + 0}}{2} = 4\]
I3–\[\frac{{7 + 2 - 0 + 1}}{2} = 5\]
XeF2\[\frac{{8 + 2 - 0 + 0}}{2} = 5\]
H2O\[\frac{{6 + 2 - 0 + 0}}{2} = 4\]
PCl5\[\frac{{5 + 5 - 0 + 0}}{2} = 5\]
XeO2F2\[\frac{{8 + 2 - 0 + 0}}{2} = 5\]
SO42–\[\frac{{6 + 0 - 0 + 2}}{2} = 4\]
NH4+\[\frac{{5 + 4 - 1 + 0}}{2} = 4\]

Thus, except I3–, XeF2 and PCl5 all have 4 hybrid orbitals on central atom.

Question:
The solubility of solid AgCN is affected by pH of solution due to hydrolysis of CN– ion.
AgCN(s) \[ \rightleftharpoons \] Ag+(aq) + CN–(aq)
CN–(aq) + H2O \[ \rightleftharpoons \] HCN + OH–
Suppose the solubility of AgCN is (x) × 10–y at [H+] = 3 × 10–10 M. Find the value of (y – x).
Given: Ksp(AgCN) = 10–16, Ka(HCN) = 10–10

(x and y both are single digit integers)
Solution:
\[AgCN\left( s \right) \rightleftharpoons \mathop {A{g^ + }\left( {aq} \right)}\limits_S + \mathop {C{N^ - }\left( {aq} \right)}\limits_{S - x} ; K_{sp} \]
\[\mathop {C{N^ - }\left( {aq} \right)}\limits_{S - x} + {H_2}O \rightleftharpoons \mathop {HCN}\limits_x + \mathop {O{H^ - }}\limits_{\frac{1}{3} \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}; \frac{K_w}{K_a} = 10^{-4} \]
${10^{ - 16}} = \left( S \right)\left( {S - x} \right)$
${10^{ - 4}} = \frac{{\left( x \right)\left( {{{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}}{{S - x}}\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 3\left( {S - x} \right) = x$
$\Rightarrow 3S = 4x$
$\Rightarrow S = \frac{4}{3}x$
$S - x = \frac{4}{3}x - x = \frac{1}{3}x$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{4}{3}x} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{3}x} \right) = {10^{ - 16}}$
$4{x^2} = 9 \times {10^{ - 16}}$
$x = \frac{3}{2} \times {10^{ - 8}}$
$ \Rightarrow S = 2 \times {10^{ - 8}}$
Ans.: $\left( {8 - 2} \right) = 6$

Question:
Raw milk sours in about 4 hours at 27oC, but in about 40 hours in a refrigerator at 7oC. What is activation energy (in KJ) for souring milk? Report the answer to the nearest integer.
[Given R = 8.3 J/mole-k, lnx = 2.3 log x]
Solution:
\[{\text{K}} \propto \frac{1}{{\text{t}}}\]
\[\frac{{{{\text{K}}_2}}}{{{{\text{K}}_1}}} = \frac{{{{\text{t}}_1}}}{{{{\text{t}}_2}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{{\text{K}}_2}}}{{{{\text{K}}_1}}} = \frac{4}{{40}}\]
\[\log \left( {\frac{{{{\text{K}}_2}}}{{{{\text{K}}_1}}}} \right) = \frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{2.3{\text{R}}}}\left( {\frac{1}{{300}} - \frac{1}{{280}}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \log {10^{ - 1}} = \frac{{{{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}}}}{{2.3{\text{R}}}}\left( {\frac{{ - 20}}{{300 \times 280}}} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow {{\text{E}}_{\text{a}}} = \frac{{2.3 \times 8.3 \times 300 \times 280}}{{20}}\]
⇒ Ea = 80178 J mol–1

⇒ Ea = 80.178 kJ mol–1

Question:
How many of the following radius orders are incorrect?
(A) N3– > P3–
(B) O2– > F–
(C) Ca2+ > Sr2+
(D) S– > S2–
(E) S2– > O–
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

(A) – Incorrect, as P3– has more electron shells, it will have bigger size.
(B) – Correct, both are isoelectronic, size decreases with increase in nuclear charge.
(C) – Incorrect, as Sr2+ has more electron shells, it will have bigger size.
(D) – Incorrect, as the charge on anion increases, size increases.
(E) – Correct, as S2- has more electron shells, it will have bigger size.

Question: pH of a mixture of 1 M HA \[\left( {p{K_a} = 4.2} \right)\]<sub> </sub>and 1 M NaA is 4.5. In 300 ml of buffer volume of HA is \[\left[ {\log 2 = 0.3} \right]\]
Solution:
\[pH = p{K_a} + \log \frac{{\left[ {Salt} \right]}}{{\left[ {Acid} \right]}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4.5 = 4.2 + \log \frac{{300 - x}}{x}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow 0.3 = \log \frac{{300 - x}}{x} \\
   \Rightarrow \log 2 = \log \frac{{300 - x}}{x} \\
   \Rightarrow 2 = \frac{{300 - x}}{x} \\
   \Rightarrow 2x = 300 - x \\
   \Rightarrow 3x = 300 \Rightarrow x = 100ml \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The ratio of the mass percentages of ‘C & H’ and ‘C & O’ of a saturated acyclic organic compound ‘X’ are 4 : 1 and 3 : 4 respectively. Then, the moles of oxygen gas required for complete combustion of two moles of organic compound ‘X’ is ………….. .
Solution:
Mass ratio of C : H is 4 : 1 ⇒ 12 : 3
& C : O is 3 : 4 ⇒ 12 : 16
So,
     mass, mole, mole ratio
C    12,        1,          1
H     3,         3,          3
O    16,       1,           1
Empirical formula ⇒ CH3O
as compound is satured acyclic so, molecular formula is C2H6O2.
\[\mathop {{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_6}{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits_{2\,{\text{mole}}} + \mathop {\frac{5}{2}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}}\limits_{5\,{\text{mole}}} \to 2{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{2\left( {\text{g}} \right)}} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{O}}_{\left( {\text{g}} \right)}}\]
So, required moles of O2 is ⇒ 5.

Question:
A line ‘$l$’ passing through origin is perpendicular to the lines
$\begin{gathered}
  {l_1}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + t} \right)\widehat i + \left( { - 1 + 2t} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + 2t} \right)\widehat k \\
  {l_2}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat i + \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat j + \left( {2 + s} \right)\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} $
If the co-ordinates of the point in the first octant on ‘${l_2}$’ at a distance of $\sqrt {17} $ from the point of intersection of ‘$l$’ and ‘${l_1}$’ are $\left( {a,\;b,\;c} \right),$ then $18\left( {a + b + c} \right)$ is equal to:
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

$\begin{gathered}
  {l_1}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + t} \right)\widehat i + \left( { - 1 + 2t} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + 2t} \right)\widehat k \\
  {l_2}:\overrightarrow r = \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat i + \left( {3 + 2s} \right)\widehat j + \left( {4 + s} \right)\widehat k \\
\end{gathered} $
DR of ${l_1} \equiv \left( {1,\;2,\;2} \right)$
DR of ${l_2} \equiv \left( {2,\;2,\;1} \right)$
DR of $l\left( {{\text{line}}\; \bot \;{\text{to}}\;{l_1}\;{\text{and}}\;{l_2}} \right)$
$ = \left( { - 2,\;3,\; - 2} \right)$
$\therefore \;\;l:\overrightarrow r = - 2\mu \widehat i + 3\mu \widehat j - 2\mu \widehat k$
For intersection of $l\;{\text{and}}\;{l_1}$
$\begin{gathered}
  3 + t = - 2\mu \\
   - 1 + 2t = 3\mu \\
  4 + 2t = - 2\mu \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow \;\;t = - 1\;{\text{and}}\;\mu = - 1$
$\therefore \;\;$Point of intersection $P \equiv \left( {2,\; - 3,\;2} \right)$
Let point on ${l_2}$ be $Q\left( {3 + 2s,\;3 + 2s,\;2 + s} \right)$
Given $PQ = \sqrt {17} \;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;{\left( {PQ} \right)^2} = 17$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \;\;{\left( {2s + 1} \right)^2} + {\left( {6 + 2s} \right)^2} + {\left( s \right)^2} = 17 \\
   \Rightarrow \;\;9{s^2} + 28s + 20 = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow \;\;s = - 2,\; - \frac{{10}}{9}$
$s \ne - 2$ as point lies on 1st octant.
$\therefore \;\;a = 3 + 2\left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{7}{9}$
$\begin{gathered}
  b = 3 + 2\left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{7}{9} \\
  c = 2 + \left( { - \frac{{10}}{9}} \right) = \frac{8}{9} \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore \;\;18\left( {a + b + c} \right) = 18\left( {\frac{{22}}{9}} \right) = 44$

Question: In an estimation of bromine by Carius method, 1.6 g of an organic compound gave 1.88 g of AgBr. The mass percentage of bromine in the compound is…... (Atomic mass, Ag = 108, Br = 80 g mol<sup>-1</sup>)
Solution:
Carius method
\[\% \;of\;Br = \frac{{wt\;of\;AgBr}}{{wt.\;of\;organic\;compound}} \times 100 \times \frac{{molar\;mass\;of\;Br}}{{AgBr}}\]
\[ = \frac{{1.88}}{{1.6}} \times \frac{{80}}{{188}} \times 100 = \frac{{15040}}{{300.8}} = 50\% \]

Question: If the reflection of the point $P(1, 0, 0)$ in the line \[\frac{{x - 1}}{2} = \frac{{y + 1}}{{ - 3}} = \frac{{z + 10}}{8}\] is \[{\text{R}}\left( {a,b,c} \right),\] then \[5a + b + c = \]
Solution:
Any point Q on the line is \[\left( {2r + 1,\; - 3r - 1,\;8r - 10} \right)\]
If Q is foot of the perpendicular from P on the line, then PQ is perpendicular to given line.
D.R.’s of PQ are \[2r,\; - 3r - 1,\;8r - 10\]
D.R.’s of given line are $2, -3, 8$
Condition of perpendicularity gives
\[\begin{gathered}
  \;\;\;\;4r + 9r + 3 + 64r - 80 = 0 \\
  {\text{or}}\;77r - 77 = 0 \\
  \therefore \;\;r = 1\;{\text{and}}\;{\text{hence}}\;{\text{Q}}\;{\text{is}}\;\left( {3,\; - 4,\; - 2} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
Since, \[{\text{R}}\left( {a,\;b,\;c} \right)\] is the reflection of P in the line. Hence, Q is mid-point of PR
\[\begin{gathered}
  \therefore \;\;\frac{{a + 1}}{2} = 3,\;\frac{b}{2} = - 4,\;\frac{c}{2} = - 2 \\
  \therefore \;\;\left( {a,\;b,\;c} \right) = \left( {5,\; - 8,\; - 4} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[ \Rightarrow 5a + b + c = 25 - 8 - 4 = 13\]

Question: Let $a,b,c$ be any real numbers. Suppose that there are real numbers $x,y,z$ not all zero such that $x = cy + bz,y = az + cx$ and $z = bx + ay.$ Then ${a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2} + 2abc$ is equal to
Solution:
System of equations
$x - cy - bz = 0$
$cx - y + az = 0$
$bx + ay - z = 0$
Has non trivial solution if the determinant of coefficient matrix is zero
$ \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  1&{ - c}&{ - b} \\
  c&{ - 1}&a \\
  b&a&{ - 1}
\end{array}} \right| = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 1\left( {1 - {a^2}} \right) + c\left( { - c - ab} \right) - b\left( {ca + b} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow {a^2} + {b^2} + {c^2} + 2abc = 1$

Question:
An oxidation-reduction reaction in which 3 electrons are transferred has a $\Delta G^\circ $of -17.37 kJ mol–1 at 25ºC. The value of $E_{cell}^0\left( {in\,{\text{V}}} \right)$is ……× 10–2.

Question:
The total number of monohalogenated organic products in the following (including stereoisomers) reaction is

(A is simplest optically active alkene)

When 100 ml of 80% (w/v) NaOH is mixed with certain amount of 40% (w/w) NaOH solution of density 1.2 g/ml, the percentage concentration of final solution becomes 60% (w/v). Find the molarity of final solution.
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

V1 = 100 ml, W1(NaOH) = 80 g
V2 = V ml, W2(Solution) = 1.2 v g
W2(NaOH) = 1.2 × 0.4 V
(W1 + W2)NaOH = 80 + 0.48 V
Vfinal = 100 + V ml
\[\frac{{80 + 0.48\,{\text{V}}}}{{100 + {\text{V}}}}\] × 100 = 60
20 = 0.12 V
V = \[\frac{{500}}{3}\]
\[\frac{{{{\left( {{{\text{W}}_1} + {{\text{W}}_2}} \right)}_{{\text{NaOH}}}}}}{{{\text{M}}{{\text{W}}_{{\text{NaOH}}}}}} = \frac{{80 + 0.48\,{\text{V}}}}{{40}} = 4\]
Molarity of final solution = \[\frac{4}{{{{\text{V}}_1} + {{\text{V}}_2}}}\]
\[ = \frac{4}{{100 + \frac{{500}}{3}}} \times 1000\]
= 15 M

Question: Atoms $_7{X^A}{,_8}{Y^B}$and $_9{Z^{17}}$ are such that, $_8Y$ is an isobar of $_7X$ and atom $_9{Z^{17}}$ is isotone of $_8Y$. Then sum of mass no. of X and no. of neutrons in \[Y\] will be
Solution:
Isotones have same no. of neutron:
$17 - 9 = B - 8$
$B = 16$
Isobars have same mass number. Thus, mass no. of X = A = B = 16
no. of neutrons in Y = 16 – 8 = 8.
Sum = 16+8 = 24

Question: For Freundlich adsorption isotherm, a plot of log (x/m)(y - axis) and log p (x - axis) gives a straight line. The intercept and slope for the line is 0.4771 and 2, respectively. The mass of gas, adsorbed per gram of adsorbent if the initial pressure is 0.04 atm, is ............ \[ \times \] 10–4g. (log 3 = 0.4471)
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  x/m = K{P^{1/n}} \\
  \log \left( {x/m} \right) = \log \left( K \right) + \frac{1}{n}\log \left( P \right) \\
  K = 3, \frac{1}{n} = 2 \Rightarrow n = \frac{1}{2} \\
  x/m = 3{\left( P \right)^{\frac{1}{n}}} \\
  x/m = 3 \times {\left( {4 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)^2} \\
   = 48 \times {10^{ - 4}}g \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Let [x] denote the greatest integer $ \leqslant x$. If $f\left( x \right) = \left[ x \right]\,and\,g\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|,$then the value of $f\left( {g\left( {\frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right) - g\left( {f\left( { - \frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right)$is
Solution:
Given \[f\left( x \right) = \left[ x \right]\,and\,g\left( x \right) = \left| x \right|\]
Now, $f\left( {g\left( {\frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right) = f\left( {\frac{8}{5}} \right) = \left[ {\frac{8}{5}} \right] = 1$
And $g\left( {f\left( {-\frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right) = g\left( {\left[ { - \frac{8}{5}} \right]} \right) = g\left( { - 2} \right) = 2$
$\therefore f\left( {g\left( {\frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right) - g\left( {f\left( { - \frac{8}{5}} \right)} \right) = 1 - 2 = - 1$.

Question: A box is constructed from a rectangular metal sheet of $21$ cm by $16$ cm by cutting equal squares of side $x$ cm from the corners of the sheet and then turning up the projected portions. The value of $x$ so that volume of the box is maximum is
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

The dimensions of the box after cutting equal squares of side $x$ from the corners will be
\[21 - 2x\,,\,\,16 - 2x\] and height $x.$
\[{\text{V}} = x\left( {21 - 2x} \right)\left( {16 - 2x} \right) = x\left( {336 - 74x + 4{x^2}} \right)\]
\[{\text{V}} = 4{x^3} - 74{x^2} + 336x\]
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 12{x^2} - 148x + 336\]
Consider, \[\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[12{x^2} - 148x + 336 = 0\]
\[4\left( {3{x^2} - 37x + 84} \right) = 0\]
\[3{x^2} - 37x + 84 = 0\]
\[3{x^2} - 9x - 28x + 84 = 0\]
\[\left( {3x - 28} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right) = 0\]
\[x = 3\] or \[x = \frac{{28}}{3}\] (Rejected)
\[\because \;{\text{for}}\;x = \frac{{28}}{3}\,,\,\,\,16 - 2x\] will become negative.
Now, \[\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}} = 24x - 148\]
\[{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right|_{x = 3}} = - ve\]
⇒ For \[x = 3\,,\] Volume of box is maximum.

Question:
How many of following are condensation co-polymer?
(i) Terylene, (ii) Nylon-6,6, (iii) Buna-N, (iv) Novalac
(v) Natural rubber, (vi) Bakelite, (vii) Buna-S, (viii) Glyptal
(ix) Gutta Parcha, (x) Melamine resin
Solution:
A copolymer is a polymer derived from more than one species of monomer.
Condensation polymers are any kind of polymers formed through a condensation reaction where molecules join together losing small molecules.
Novalac is a linear condensation product of phenol and formaldehyde.
Glyptal is the condensation product of Ethylene glycol and phthalic acid.
Bakelite is a condensation product of phenol and formaldehyde.
Melamine is formed by melamine and formaldehyde.
Terylene is the condensation product of ethylene glycol and Terephthalic acid.
Nylon – 6, 6 is obtained by the condensation polymerisation of adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine

Question: The line \[2x - y + 1 = 0\] is tangent to the circle at the point \[\left( {2,5} \right)\] and the centre of the circle lies on \[x - 2y = 4\]. The square of the radius of the circle is
Solution:
Let O is the centre of the circle and the point of contact of tangent is A
Slope of line \[OA = - \frac{1}{2}\]
Equation of OA is \[(y - 5) = - \frac{1}{2}(x - 2)\]
Or \[x + 2y = 12\]
The intersection of \[x + 2y = 12\] with \[x - 2y = 4\] will give coordinates of centre which are \[(8,2)\]
\[\therefore \,\,\,r = OA = \sqrt {{{(8 - 2)}^2} + {{(2 - 5)}^2}} = 3\sqrt 5 \]

Question: An octapeptide (Mol. wt. = 516 g) on complete hydrolysis gives an alanine (Mol. mass =89 g). Alanine contributes 41.59% to total weight of hydrolysed product. How many numbers of alanine units are present in octapeptide?
Solution:
Molecular weight of octapeptide = 516 g/mole 
Total bonds to be hydrolysed = 8 – 1 =7 
Total weight of H2O added = 7 × 18 = 126 g/mol 
Total weight of hydrolysed product = 126 + 516 = 642 g 
Total weight of alanine in product = 624 × 41.59/100 = 267 g  
Molecular weight of alanine = 89 g/mole 
Number of alanine unit = 267/89 = 3

Question: If \[3 + \frac{1}{4}(3 + d) + \frac{1}{{{4^2}}}(3 + 2d)\] + .........  to \[\infty  = 8,\] then the value of d is

Two solutions A and B each of 100 L was made by dissolving 4 g of NaOH and 9.8 g of \[{H_2}S{O_4}\]in water, respectively. The pH of the resulting solution obtained from mixing 40 L of Solution A and 10 L of Solution B is $x$. Find 10$x$
Solution:
Moles of NaOH $ = \frac{4}{{40}} = 0.1$ 

Molarity of solution $A = \frac{{{\text{moles}}}}{{{\text{volume}}}} = \frac{{0.1}}{{100}} = {10^{ - 3}}$ 

Moles of ${H_2}S{O_4} = \frac{{9.8}}{{98}} = 0.1$ 

Molarity of solution $B = \frac{{{\text{moles}}}}{{{\text{volume}}}} = \frac{{0.1}}{{100}} = {10^{ - 3}}$ 

40 litre of solution A contains, 

Moles of $NaOH = 40 \times {10^{ - 3}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 2}}$ 

10 litre of solution B contains, 

Moles of ${H_2}S{O_4} = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} = {10^{ - 2}}$ 

Total volume finally = 40 litre + 10 litre

Moles of OH– = moles of NaOH = 2 × 10–2

$\left[ {O{H^ - }} \right] = \frac{{{\text{moles of O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }}}{{{\text{Total volume}}}} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{50}} = 4 \times {10^{ - 4}}$ 

$\left[ {{H^ + }} \right] = \frac{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 11}}$ 

$pH =  - \log \left[ {{H^ + }} \right] =  - \log \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}} \right) = 10.6$

Question: The length of the chord of the circle (x – 3)<sup>2</sup> + (y – 5)<sup>2</sup> = 80 cut off by the line 3x – 4y – 9 = 0 is
Solution:
Distance of centre from the given line,
\[CM = \frac{{\left| {3 \times 3 - 4 \times 5 - 9} \right|}}{{\sqrt {{{(3)}^2} + {{( - 4)}^2}} }} = \frac{{20}}{5} = 4\]
\[\therefore AM = \sqrt {A{C^2} - C{M^2}} = \sqrt {80 - 16} = 8\]
∴ length of chord AB = 2AM = 16.

Question:
Polyethene, Polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene, Bakelite, Urea-formaldehyde, Nylon 6,6, terylene, neoprene  PHBV, Nylon 2-nylon 6 etc. are the some daily used polymers.
Give the answer of below questions on basis of these polymers.

(1) In the above polymers number of polymers which are thermoplastic polymers and made by addition polymerization reaction are =  X
(2) The number of polymers which are formed by condensation polymerization reaction and are thermosetting polymers = Y
(3) The number of polymers which are copolymer and biodegradable = Z

what is the value of Z + Y - X
Solution:
(1) Polyethene, Polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene are thermoplastic polymers and made by addition polymerization reaction

X = 3

(2) The number of polymers which are formed by condensation polymerization reaction and are thermosetting polymers are  Bakelite and Urea-formaldehyde Y = 2

(3) PHBV, Nylon 2-nylon 6 are  biodegradable and copolymer Z = 2

hence   Z + Y - X = 2 + 2 - 3 =1

Question: The block of mass ‘m’ slides on a wedge of mass ‘m’ which is free to move on a horizontal smooth ground. If angle of inclination of wedge is 45°, then magnitude of acceleration of wedge is$\frac{{10}}{x}$ m/s2. Then the value of x is : (All surfaces are smooth and g = 10 m/s2)
Solution:
FBD of block from wedge frame

$$\frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \frac{{ma}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = m{a_1}$$......(i)

\[{a_{cm}},{\text{horizontal}} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \left( {\frac{{{a_1}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} - a} \right)m + m\left( { - a} \right) = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{a_1}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2a\]                       (II)

put $${a_1}$$ in eq (I)

\[\frac{{mg}}{{\sqrt 2 }} + \frac{{ma}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = m2\sqrt 2 a\]

\[ \Rightarrow mg + ma = 4ma\]

\[ \Rightarrow a = \frac{g}{3} = \frac{{10}}{3}m/{s^2}\]

Question: The probability of a man hitting a target is \[\frac{1}{{10}}\]. The least number of shots required, so that the probability of his hitting the target at least once is greater than \[\,\frac{1}{4},\] is….
Solution:
Probability of hitting $P(H) = $\[\frac{1}{{10}}\];
Probability of missing $P(M) = $\[\frac{9}{{10}}\]
We have, 1- (probability of all shots result in failure) > \[\frac{1}{4}\]
\[ 1 - P{(M)^n}{\mathbf{ > }}\frac{1}{4}\]
\[{\mathbf{ }}1 - {\left( {\frac{9}{{10}}} \right)^n}{\mathbf{ > }}\frac{1}{4}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \,{\left( {\frac{9}{{10}}} \right)^n}{\mathbf{ < }}\frac{3}{4} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,n \geqslant 3 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The probability of a man hitting a target is \[\frac{1}{{10}}\]. The least number of shots required, so that the probability of his hitting the target at least once is greater than \[\,\frac{1}{4}\], is….

Probability of missing P(M) = \[\frac{9}{{10}}\]

\[ = 1 - P{(M)^n}{\mathbf{ > }}\frac{1}{4}\]
\[{\mathbf{ = }}1 - {\left( {\frac{9}{{10}}} \right)^n}{\mathbf{ > }}\frac{1}{4}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \,{\left( {\frac{9}{{10}}} \right)^n}{\mathbf{ < }}\frac{3}{4} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,n \geqslant 3 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Two bodies of the same mass are moving with the same speed, but in different directions in a plane. They have a completely inelastic collision and move together thereafter with a final speed which is half of their initial speed. The angle between the initial velocities of the two bodies (in degree) is ________.
Solution:
\[\therefore \]In Horizontal Direction
By Momentum conservation.
\[mv + mv\cos \theta = 2m\frac{v}{2}\cos \alpha \]
\[1 + \cos \theta = \cos \alpha \] … (1)
In vertical direction
By Momentum conservation.
\[0 + mv\sin \theta = 2m\frac{v}{2}\sin \alpha \]
\[\sin \theta = \sin \alpha \]
\[1 + \cos \theta = \sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}\theta } \]
\[\theta = 120^\circ \]

Question:
At \[{67^ \circ }C\] and 1 bar pressure, dinitrogen tetra oxide is 50% dissociated into nitrogen dioxide. \[\Delta {G^ \circ }\] (J/mol) for the process:
\[2N{O_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons {N_2}{O_4}\left( g \right)\] is –
\[\left( {R = \frac{{25}}{3}J/K.mol,\ln 2 = 0.7,\ln 3 = 1.1} \right)\]
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {N_2}{O_4}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons 2N{O_2}\left( g \right) \\
  {P_0}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} - \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[{P_0}\left( {1 - \alpha } \right){\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} 2{P_0}\alpha \] where \[\alpha = \frac{1}{2}\]
\[{P_0}\left( {1 - \alpha } \right){\kern 1pt} = 1\]bar \[ \Rightarrow {P_0} = \frac{2}{3}\] bar
\[{K_p} = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right)}^2}}}{{\left( {\frac{2}{6}} \right)}}\] bar \[ = \frac{4}{3}\] bar
For reverse reaction
\[2N{O_2}\left( g \right) \rightleftharpoons {N_2}{O_4}\left( g \right)\]; \[{K_p} = \frac{3}{4}ba{r^{ - 1}}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \Delta G = - RT\ln {K_p} = - 340 \times \frac{{25}}{3}\ln \frac{3}{4} \\
  \Delta G = + 340 \times \frac{{25}}{3}\left( {2\ln 2 - \ln 3} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\Delta G = 340 \times \frac{{25}}{3} \times 0.3 = 850\] J/mol

Question: Calculate the drift speed (in m/s and to the nearest integer) of the electron when 1A of current exists in a wire of cross-section \[2 \times {10^{ - 11}}{m^2}\]. The number of free electron in $1c{m^3}$ of the material of the wire is $7.8 \times {10^{22}}.$

$n = $ free electron density,
$ = 7.8 \times {10^{22}}per\,c{m^3}$ (Given)
$ = \left( {7.8 \times {{10}^{22}}} \right)\left( {{{10}^6}} \right)per\,{m^3}$
$ = 7.8 \times {10^{28}}per\,{m^3}$
From $i = neA{v_d},$ we get
${v_d} = \frac{i}{{neA}}$
Substituting the value in SI units we have,
$\begin{gathered}
  {v_d} = \frac{1}{{\left( {7.8 \times {{10}^{28}}} \right)\left( {1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}} \right)\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}} \right)}} \\ = 4   m/s  

\end{gathered} $

Question: What should be the minimum atomic number of a hydrogen-like species X so that its 4<sup>th</sup> orbit fits inside or exactly matches with the 1<sup>st</sup> Bohr orbit?
Solution:
For a hydrogen like species, $r = \frac{a_0 n^2}{Z}$, where $a_0$ is the radius of the first Bohr orbit of hydrogen atom.

For the orbit to fit inside the first Bohr orbit, $\frac{a_0 n^2}{Z}$ ≤ $a_0$

$n^2$ ≤ $Z$

$4^2$ ≤ $Z$

$Z$ ≥ $16$

Question: An open glass tube is immersed in mercury in such a way that a length of 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The open end of the tube is then closed and sealed and the tube is raised vertically up by additional 46 cm. What will be the length (cm) of the air column above mercury in the tube now? (atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg)
Solution:
For air trapped in tube, $${P_1}{V_1} = {P_2}{V_2}$$
$${P_1} = {P_{atm}} = \rho g76$$
$${V_1} = A \times 8$$
[$$\because A = $$ Area of cross-section]
$${P_2} = {P_{atm}} - \rho g\left( {54 - x} \right)$$
$$ = \rho g\left( {22 + x} \right)$$
$${V_2} = A \times x$$
At constant temperature,

$${P_1}{V_1} = {P_2}{V_2}$$

$$\rho g76.A8 = \rho g\left( {22 + x} \right)Ax$$

$${x^2} + 22x - 76 \times 8 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 16\,cm$$

Question: The velocity of projection of a body is increased by 2%. Other factors remaining unchanged. What will be the percentage change in the maximum height attained?
Solution:
$${h_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}$$

$$\frac{{\Delta {h_{\max }}}}{{{h_{\max }}}} = 2\frac{{\Delta u}}{u}$$

$$\% \,{\text{change}}\,\,{\text{in}}\,\,{h_{\max }} = \frac{{\Delta {h_{\max }}}}{{{h_{\max }}}} \times 100\% $$

$$= 2\frac{{\Delta u}}{u}\times 100\%$$

$ = 2 \times \frac{2}{{100}} \times 100\% $

= 4%

Question: Length of a uniform rod varies with temperature (T) (in <sup>0</sup>C) as \[L = {L_0}\left( {1 + aT + b{T^2}} \right)\], where L<sub>0</sub> is length at 0<sup>0</sup> C. Thermal coeﬃcient of linear expansion(α)for this rod at 5<sup>0</sup>C is \[\frac{{a + \mu b}}{{1 + 5a + 25b}}\]. Find \[\mu \]
Solution:
\[\ln L = \ln {L_0} + \ln \left( {1 + aT + b{T^2}} \right)\]

\[\frac{1}{L}\frac{{dL}}{{dT}} = \frac{{a + 2bT}}{{1 + aT + b{T^2}}}\]

Put T = 5

Question: If the order and the degree of the differential equation of the family of circles whose centres lie on the X axis are respectively equal to \[p\] and \[q\], then \[{p^2} + {q^2} = \,\]_____.
Solution:
The equation of the family of circles whose centres lie on the X axis have two arbitrary constants, namely, the abscissae of the centres (say \[\alpha \]) and the radii of the circles (say \[r\]).
So, their equations will be of the form \[{\left( {x - \alpha } \right)^2} + {y^2} = {r^2}\].
Differentiating, we get \[2\left( {x - \alpha } \right) + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\] or \[\left( {x - \alpha } \right) + y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\].
On differentiating again, we get \[1 + y\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)^2} = 0\].
Clearly, \[p = 2\] and \[q = 1\], so \[{p^2} + {q^2} = 4 + 1 = 5\].

Question: The breaking stress for a metal is 7.8 × 10<sup>9</sup> Nm<sup>–</sup><sup>2</sup>. The density of the metal is 7800 kg m<sup>–</sup><sup>3</sup>. If g = 10 N kg<sup>–</sup><sup>1</sup>, Find the maximum length of the wire (in km) made of this metal which may be suspended without breaking:
Solution:
If L is the maximum length of the wire that can be suspended. Then F = mg, where m = ρ LA (where, A = cross- sectional area)
\[\therefore \,\,{\text{Maximum}}\,\,{\text{stress}}\, = \frac{{mg}}{A} = g.\frac{{\rho LA}}{A} = \rho Lg\]
Given, breaking stress $${\sigma _b} = 7.8 \times {10^9}{\mkern 1mu} N{m^{ - 2}}$$
\[ \Rightarrow L = 7.8 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{{7800 \times 10}}\]
\[{\text{or}}\,\,{\text{L = 1}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{5}}}m = 100km\]

Question:
If $A = \left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  1&2&2 \\
  2&1&2 \\
  2&2&1
\end{array}} \right)$ and $A^{2}-4A-5I=O$, where $I$ and $O$ are the unit matrix and the null matrix of order 3 respectively. If $15{A^{ - 1}} = \lambda \left( {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  { - 3}&2&2 \\
  2&{ - 3}&2 \\
  2&2&{ - 3}
\end{array}} \right)$, then find the value of $\lambda $.
Solution:
$A^{2}-4A-5I=O$
Multiplying by $A^{ - 1}$  both sides,
\[\begin{gathered}
  {A^{ - 1}}\left[ {{A^2} - 4A - 5I} \right] = {A^{ - 1}}0 \\
  A - 4I - 5{A^{ - 1}} = 0 \\
  A - 4I = 5{A^{ - 1}} \\
\end{gathered} \]
${A^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{5}\left( {A - 4I} \right) = \frac{1}{5}\left( {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  1&2&2 \\
  2&1&2 \\
  2&2&1
\end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  4&0&0 \\
  0&4&0 \\
  0&0&4
\end{array}} \right]} \right)$
${A^{ - 1}} = \frac{1}{5}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}}
  { - 3}&2&2 \\
  2&{ - 3}&2 \\
  2&2&{ - 3}
\end{array}} \right]$

A rod of mass 6 kg and length 2 m, is rotating with an angular speed 1 rad/s about a point ‘O’ such that, the rod always makes an angle of 30° with vertical. What is the angular momentum of rod about ‘O’ [in SI units]?
Solution:
$dL = \left( {dm} \right)\left( {\omega .x\sin \theta } \right)x$
$\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{m}{\ell }dx.\omega {x^2}\sin \theta \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,dL = \frac{{m\omega \sin \theta }}{\ell }\int\limits_0^\ell {{x^2}dx} \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow L = \frac{{m{\ell ^2}\omega \sin \theta }}{3}$
Putting value, we get L = 4

Question:
If \[\left| {{z_1}} \right| = 1,\left| {{z_2}} \right| = 2,\left| {{z_3}} \right| = 3\] and \[\left| {9{z_1}{z_2} + 4{z_3}{z_1} + {z_2}{z_3}} \right| = 6\], then value of
\[\left| {{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}} \right|\]is
Solution:
\[\because \,\,\left| {{z_1}} \right| = 1 \Rightarrow {\left| {{z_1}} \right|^2} = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow {z_1}{\bar z_1} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{z_1}}} = {\bar z_1}\]
\[\left| {{z_2}} \right| = 2 \Rightarrow {\left| {{z_2}} \right|^2} = 4 \Rightarrow {z_2}{\bar z_2} = 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{4}{{{z_2}}} = {\bar z_2}\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\left| {{z_3}} \right| = 3 \Rightarrow {\left| {{z_3}} \right|^2} = 9\]
\[ \Rightarrow {z_3}{\bar z_3} = 9 \Rightarrow \frac{9}{{{z_3}}} = {\bar z_3}\]
and given \[\left| {9{z_1}{z_2} + 4{z_3}{z_1} + {z_2}{z_3}} \right| = 6\]
\[\left| {{z_1}.\,{z_2}.\,{z_3}} \right|\left| {\frac{9}{{{z_3}}} + \frac{4}{{{z_2}}} + \frac{1}{{{z_1}}}} \right| = 6\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left| {{z_1}} \right|\left| {{z_2}} \right|\left| {{z_3}} \right|\left| {{{\bar z}_3} + {{\bar z}_2} + {{\bar z}_1}} \right| = 6\] \[\left[ {\because \,\,\frac{1}{{{z_1}}} = {{\bar z}_1},\frac{4}{{{z_2}}} = {{\bar z}_2}\,\,{\text{and }}\,\frac{9}{{{z_3}}} = {{\bar z}_3}\,} \right]\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3\left| {\overline {{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}} } \right| = 6\]
\[\therefore \,\,\left| {{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}} \right| = 1\] \[\left[ {\because \,\,\left| {\bar z} \right| = \left| z \right|} \right]\]

Question: What is the maximum number of hydrogen bonds one molecule of H<sub>2</sub>O can form?
Solution:
The two hydrogen atoms of the water molecule can form hydrogen bonds with other oxygen atoms. Due to the presence of two lone pairs on the oxygen of the water molecule, it can also attract two other hydrogen atoms to form hydrogen bonds. 
Hence, four possible hydrogen bonds per water molecule.

Question: M grams of steam at 100°C is mixed with 200 gm of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at 40°C [heat of vaporization of water is 540 cal/g and heat of fusion of ice is 80 cal/g], the value of M is______.

Let $F\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right)g\left( x \right)h\left( x \right)$ for all real x, where $f\left( x \right),g\left( x \right)\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,h\left( x \right)$ are differentiable functions. At some point ${x_0},$ $F'\left( {{x_0}} \right) = 21\,F\left( {{x_0}} \right),\,\,\,f'\left( {{x_0}} \right) = 4f\left( {{x_0}} \right),$ $g'\left( {{x_0}} \right) = - 7g\left( {{x_0}} \right)$ and \[h'\left( {{x_0}} \right) = kh\left( {{x_0}} \right).\] Then the value of k is …..
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  F\left( x \right) = f\left( x \right)g\left( x \right)h\left( x \right) \\
   \Rightarrow F'\left( x \right) = f'\left( x \right)g\left( x \right)h\left( x \right) + f\left( x \right)g'\left( x \right)h\left( x \right) + f\left( x \right)g\left( x \right)h'\left( x \right) \\
\end{gathered} $
$\therefore \,\,F'\left( {{x_0}} \right) = f'\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) + f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g'\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) + f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h'\left( {{x_0}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,21\,F\left( {{x_0}} \right) = 4f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) + f\left( {{x_0}} \right) \times \left( { - 7} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) + f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)kh\left( {{x_0}} \right)$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,21\,f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) = \left( {4 - 7 + k} \right)f\left( {{x_0}} \right)g\left( {{x_0}} \right)h\left( {{x_0}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow k = 24 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If $\lambda $ is the common positive root of the equations ${x^2} - ax + 12 = 0$, ${x^2} - bx + 15 = 0$ and ${x^2} - \left( {a + b} \right)x + 36 = 0$, and the value of $\cos x + \cos 2x + \cos 3x$ is equal to $\lambda $, then the value of $\sin x + \sin 2x + \sin 3x$ is equal to _____.
Solution:
Given $\lambda $ is the common positive root of the equations ${x^2} - ax + 12 = 0$, ${x^2} - bx + 15 = 0$ and ${x^2} - \left( {a + b} \right)x + 36 = 0$.
So, we have ${\lambda ^2} - a\lambda + 12 = 0$, ${\lambda ^2} - b\lambda + 15 = 0$ and ${\lambda ^2} - \left( {a + b} \right)\lambda + 36 = 0$.
Solving these equations, we get ${\lambda ^2} = 9\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,\lambda = 3\,\,\,\,\,\left( {\because \,\,\,\lambda > 0} \right)$.
So, $\cos x + \cos 2x + \cos 3x = 3$, which is possibly only if $\cos x = \cos 2x = \cos 3x = 1$, for which $\sin x = \sin 2x = \sin 3x = 0$, so \[\sin x + \sin 2x + \sin 3x = 0\].

Question:
A linear harmonic oscillator of force constant $2 \times {10^6}\,N{m^{ - 1}}$ and amplitude 0.01 m has a total mechanical energy of 160 J , then its potential energy 

<!-StartFragment->(in joule)<!-EndFragment->  at mean position is
Solution:
Maximum kinetic energy given by 

$\frac{1}{2}k{A^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {10^6} \times {\left( {0.01} \right)^2} = 100\,J$

Total Mechanical Energy of the motion is 160 J so,
At mean position,
$U = 60\,J$and \[K = 100\,J\]
Hence,the required answer (potential energy at the mean position ) is  60 J

Question: Unpolarised light of intensity 32 Wm<sup>-2</sup> passes through three polarisers such that the transmission axis of the last polariser is perpendicular to the first. If the intensity of the emerging light is 3 Wm<sup>-2</sup>, what is the minimum angle (degrees) between the transmission axes of the first two polarisers?
Solution:
Light passes through first polarizer

$${\text{I}} = {{\text{I}}_0}/2 = 16{\text{W}}{{\text{m}}^{ - 2}}$$

If angle between $${P_1}$$ and $${P_2} = \theta $$

and angle between $${P_2}$$ and $${P_3} = \phi $$

$$\therefore $$   $${\kern 1pt} \theta  + \phi  = 90^\circ $$

$$\therefore {{\text{I}}_1} = {{\text{I}}_0}{\cos ^2}\theta $$ and

$${{\text{I}}_2} = {{\text{I}}_1}{\cos ^2}\phi  = {{\text{I}}_0}{\cos ^2}\theta  \cdot {\cos ^2}\phi  = 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\text{I}}_0}{\cos ^2}\theta  \cdot {\cos ^2}\left( {90 - \theta } \right) = 3 \Rightarrow 16{\cos ^2}\theta {\sin ^2}\theta  = 3$$

$${\left( {2\cos \theta \sin \theta } \right)^2} = \frac{3}{4}$$

$$\sin 2\theta  = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}$$

$$\sin 2\theta  = 60^\circ $$

$$\theta  = 30^\circ $$

Question: Two blocks of masses 2 kg and 4 kg are connected by a metal wire passing through a frictionless pulley as shown in the Figure. The breaking stress of the metal is $\frac{{80}}{{3\pi }} \times {10^6}N{m^{ - 2}},$ If g = 10 ms−2, the minimum radius of the wire (in mm) which will not break when masses are suspended is ___
Solution:
Tension in the metal wire, $$T = \frac{{2{m_1}{m_2}}}{{{m_1} + {m_2}}}g$$

$$ = \frac{{2 \times 2 \times 4 \times 10}}{{2 + 4}} = \frac{8}{3}N$$

Breaking stress of the wire $$ = \frac{{80}}{{3\pi }} \times {10^6}N{m^{ - 2}}$$

Force per unit area in the wire due to masses $${m_1}$$ and $${m_2}$$ $$ = \frac{{\frac{{80}}{3}}}{{\pi {r^2}}} = \frac{{80}}{{3\pi {r^2}}}N{m^{ - 2}}$$

Where r is the radius of the wire.

$$\frac{{80}}{{3\pi {r^2}}} = \frac{{80}}{{3\pi }} \times {10^6} \Rightarrow {r^2} = \frac{1}{{{{10}^6}}}$$

$$\therefore r = \frac{1}{{{{10}^3}}}m = {10^{ - 3}}m = 1\,mm$$

Question: Two friends A and B have equal number of daughters. There are three cinema tickets which are to be distributed among the daughters of A and B. The probability that all the tickets go to daughters of A is 1/20. The number of daughters each of them have is
Solution:
Let $x$ be the number of daughters to each of A, B
Probability that all the tickets go to daughters of A is
$\frac{{^x{C_3}}}{{^{2x}{C_3}}} = \frac{{x\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right)}}{{2x\left( {2x - 1} \right)\left( {2x - 2} \right)}} = \frac{{x - 2}}{{4\left( {2x - 1} \right)}}$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,\frac{{x - 2}}{{4\left( {2x - 1} \right)}} = \frac{1}{{20}} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,5x - 10 = 2x - 1 \\
\end{gathered} $
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,3x = 9 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,x = 3 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The number of points where \[f(x) = \operatorname{sgn} ({x^2} - 3x + 2) + [x - 3],x \in [0,4],\]is discontinuous is (where [.] denotes the greatest integer function) _______.
Solution:
\[\operatorname{sgn} ({x^2} - 3x + 2)\]is discontinuous when \[{x^2} - 3x + 2 = 0\]or x = 1, 2.
[x - 3] = [x] − 3 is discontinuous at x = 1, 2, 3, 4.
Thus, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 3, 4.
Now, both \[\operatorname{sgn} ({x^2} - 3x + 2)\]and [x - 3] are discontinuous at x = 1 and 2.
Then, f(x) may be continuous at x = 1 and 2.
But f(1) = -2 and \[f({1^ + }) = - 1 + 1 - 3 = - 3\]
Thus, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 1.
Also, f(2) = -1 and \[f({2^ + }) = 1 - 1 = 0\].
Hence, f(x) is discontinuous at x = 2 also.

Question:
The work done in adiabatic compression of 2 moles of an ideal monatomic gas by the constant external pressure of 2 atm starting from an initial pressure of 1 atm and an initial temperature of 300 K is (in cal):
[R = 2 cal/mol-K]
Solution:
"THIS IS A HIGH WEIGHTAGE TOPIC"

To calculate the work done, We need to know the final temperature.
T1 = 300K, P 1 =1 atm, n = 2 mole , P2 = 2 atm 
W = ΔU = n Cv (T2−T 1) = − P2 (V 2 − V 1)
For a monatomic gas, Cv = $\frac{3}{2}$ R
⇒$\frac{3}{2}$ nR(T2​−T 1) = −P2 (V2−V1)
$\frac{3}{2}$ nR(T2−T1) = − P2 (nR (T2 /P2) − nR (T1 / P1))
T2 = 0.4(2 + 1.5)300 = 420K
Work done, W=nCvΔT = 2 × 3/2R × (420−300)
⇒W = 2 × 3/2 × 2 × 120 = 720 cal

Question: Two squares are chosen at random on a chess board. If the probability that they may have a contact at corner is $\frac{\mu }{{144}}$ then $\mu $ should be equal to
Solution:
Total cases of choosing two squares on a chess board = 64 × 63
Favourable cases = $4 \times 1 + 24 \times 2 + 36 \times 4 = 4 \times 49$
Required probability = $\frac{{4 \times 49}}{{64 \times 63}} = \frac{7}{{144}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\mu = 7$

Question: The given fig. shows a coil bent with AB=BC =CD=DE = EF = FG=GH=HA= 1m and carrying current 1A. There exists in space a vertical uniform magnetic field of 2T in the y-direction. Then find out the magnitude of torque on the loop

Question: Die A has four red and two white faces whereas die B has two red and four white faces. A coin is flipped once. If it falls a head, the game continues by throwing die A, if it falls tail then die B is to be used. If the probability that die A is used is $\frac{{32}}{{33}}$ when it is given that red turns up every time in first n throws, then the value of n is _____.
Solution:
Let R be the event that a red face appears in each of the first n throws.

E1 : Die A is used when head has already fallen

E2 : Die B is used when tail has already fallen.

$\therefore \,\,P\left( {\frac{R}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = {\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right)^n}$ and $P\left( {\frac{R}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = {\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)^n}$

As per the given condition

\[\frac{{P({E_1}).P(R/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1}).P(R/{E_1})\, + \,P({E_2}).P(R/{E_2})}}\, = \,\frac{{32}}{{33}}\]

⇒ \[\frac{{1/2.\,{{(2/3)}^n}}}{{\frac{1}{2}.\,{{\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right)}^n}\, + \,\frac{1}{2}{{\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)}^n}}}\, = \,\frac{{32}}{{33}}\]

⇒ \[\frac{{{2^n}}}{{{2^n} + 1}}\, = \,\,\frac{{32}}{{33}}\] ⇒ n = 5.

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: Total probability law and bayes' theorem

Question: A stone falls from rest. The distance covered by the stone in the last second of its motion equals the distance covered by it during the first three seconds of its motion. How long (in s) does the stone take to reach the ground?
Solution:
Let n seconds be the time taken by the stone to reach the ground. The distance covered in the last second distance covered in n seconds – distance covered in $\left( {n - 1} \right)$ seconds
$ = \frac{1}{2}g{n^2} - \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {n - 1} \right)^2}$
The distance covered in the first 3 seconds $ = \frac{1}{2}g \times {\left( 3 \right)^2}$
It is given that
$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{1}{2}g{n^2} - \frac{1}{2}g{\left( {n - 1} \right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}g \times {\left( 3 \right)^2} \\
  {\text{or }}{n^2} - {\left( {n - 1} \right)^2} = 9 \\
\end{gathered} $
Which gives $n = 5s$

Question:
In an experiment with 15 observations on x, the following results were available:
\[\sum {{x^2} = 2830,\;\sum {x = 170} } \]
One observation, 20, was found to be wrong and was replaced by the correct value 30. Then the corrected variance is _______.
Solution:
\[\sum {x = 170,\;\sum {{x^2} = 2830} } \]
The increase in \[\sum x \] is 10, then
\[\sum {x'} = 170 + 10 = 180\]
The increase in \[\sum {{x^2}\;{\text{is}}\;} of\,\,900 - 400 = 500,\] then
\[\sum {x{'^2} = 2830 + 500 = 3330} \]
Variance \[ = \frac{1}{n}\sum {x{'^2} - {{\left( {\frac{1}{n}\sum {x'} } \right)}^2}} \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = \frac{1}{{15}} \times 3330 - {\left( {\frac{1}{{15}} \times 180} \right)^2} \\
   = 222 - 144 = 78 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Binding energy per nucleon of a fixed nucleus X<sup>A</sup> is 6 MeV. It absorbs a neutron moving with KE= 2 MeV, and converts into Y, emitting a photon of energy 1 MeV. If the Binding Energy per nucleon of Y (in MeV) is found to be \[\frac{{(\lambda A - 1)}}{{(A + 1)}}\].Find the value of \[\lambda \]. A is mass number of X
Solution:
BE of X = 6A
BE of Y = 6A – 2 + 1 = 6A – 1
[Because absorption of energy decreases BE and release of energy increases BE] In Y nucleus there A + 1 nucleus.
\[\therefore \,\,\frac{{BE}}{{{\text{nucleon}}}} = \frac{{6A - 1}}{{A + 1}}\]

Question: A coil carrying a current of I = 10mA is placed in uniform magnetic field so that its axis coincides with the field direction (out of the plane of paper). The coil has only one turn and is made of copper. The diameter of the wire is 0.1 mm, the radius of coil is R = 3 cm. An approximate external field B will rupture the coil is found to be  y×10<sup>3</sup>T. Find y (write only magnitude as the nearest integer) . Breaking stress = 3×10<sup>8</sup>N/m<sup>2</sup>.
Solution:
Take a small element of length \[dl\] of the  coil carrying current I. It is placed perpendicular to the Magnetic field induction \[\vec B.\]

It experience a force Bidl. Due to this force there will be tension T in the wire.

If \[d\alpha \] is the angle formed by the element at the centre O, then for equilibrium,

\[BIdl = 2\left( {T\sin d\alpha /2} \right) = 2Td\alpha /2 = Td\alpha \]

\[\left[ {\because d\alpha \,{\text{is}}\,{\text{small,hence}}\,\sin \left( {d\alpha /2} \right) = d\alpha /2} \right]\]

\[\therefore \,T = \frac{{BIdl}}{{d\alpha }} = BIr\left[ {\because r = dl/d\alpha } \right]\]

Now stress on wire, \[S = \frac{T}{{\left( {\pi {D^2}/4} \right)}} = \frac{{BIr}}{{\left( {\pi {D^2}/4} \right)}}\]

If \[{B_m}\] is the maximum value of magnetic fied induction then breaking stress for rupture of wire is 

\[{S_m} = \frac{{{B_m}Ir}}{{\left( {\pi {D^2}} \right)/4}}{\text{or}}\,\,{B_m} = \frac{{\pi {D^2}{S_m}}}{{4Ir}}\]

\[\therefore {B_m} = \pi  \times \frac{{{{\left( {0.1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {3 \times {{10}^8}} \right)}}{{4 \times \left( {10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) \times \left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \right)}}\]

\[ =  \sim \,8 \times {10^3}T\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Magnetic force on a current carrying straight wire

Question: The minimum energy required to remove the electron from metal surface is 7.50 × 10<sup>–19</sup> J. What will be the stopping potential required when this metal surface is exposed to U.V. light of λ  = 375 Å? (roundoff your answer to nearest integer)

A ball is dropped from certain height towards a smooth and fixed inclined surface of inclination 30°. Just after collision velocity of ball becomes horizontal. If coefficient of restitution is $\frac{1}{x}$ then x is
Solution:
Along the incline velocity does not changes,

\[u\cos 60^\circ  = v\cos 30^\circ \]

\[\frac{u}{v} = \frac{{\cos 30^\circ }}{{\cos 60^\circ }}\]

\[\frac{u}{v} = \sqrt 3 \]

Also, \[e = \frac{{v\sin 30^\circ }}{{u\sin 60^\circ }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} = \frac{1}{3}\]

Question: The equation of the circle which touches axes of coordinates and the line $\frac{x}{3} + \frac{y}{4} = 1$ and whose centre lies in the first quadrant is ${x^2} + {y^2} - 2\lambda x - 2\lambda y + {\lambda ^2} = 0$, where $\lambda \left( { > 1} \right)$ is equal to
Solution:
Centre of the circle is $\left( {\lambda ,\lambda } \right)$ and radius is $\lambda $.
A tangent to the circle is $4x + 4y - 12 = 0$.
By condition of tangency
$\frac{{\left| {4\lambda + 3\lambda - 12} \right|}}{5} = \lambda $
$7\lambda - 12 = \pm \,5\lambda $
$\lambda = 1,6$

Question: Number of –OH groups present in \[\beta  - \]deoxyribofuranose
If \[\left| {\bar a} \right| = 2,\] \[\left| {\bar b} \right| = 5\] and \[\left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right| = 8,\] then \[\left| {\bar a.\,\bar b} \right| = \,\]_____.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\left| {\bar a \times \bar b} \right|^2} + {\left| {\bar a.\,\bar b} \right|^2} = {\left| {\bar a} \right|^2}{\left| {\bar b} \right|^2}{\sin ^2}\theta + {\left| {\bar a} \right|^2}{\left| {\bar b} \right|^2}{\cos ^2}\theta = {\left| {\bar a} \right|^2}{\left| {\bar b} \right|^2} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{8^2} + {\left| {\bar a.\,\bar b} \right|^2} = {2^2}{5^2} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{\left| {\bar a.\,\bar b} \right|^2} = 36 \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\left| {\bar a.\,\bar b} \right| = 6 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question:
In the balanced chemical reaction, \[IO_3^ - + a{I^ - } + b{H^ + } \to c{H_2}O + d{I_2}\]
Sum of a, b, c and d is equal to:
Solution:
\[IO_3^ - \to {I_2}\]
\[{I^ - } \to {I_2}\]
Balance the charges by adding electrons to the more electropositive side until it is balanced:
\[IO_3^ - + 5{e^ - } \to {I_2}\]
\[{I^ - } \to {I_2} + {e^ - }\]
Then, balance the oxygen atoms by adding H2O to the oxygen deficient side – one molecule of H2O for every O that is deficient and balance the hydrogen by adding H+.
\[IO_3^ - + 5{e^ - } + 6{H^ + } \to {I_2} + 3{H_2}O\]
\[{I^ - } \to {I_2} + {e^ - }\]
Multiplying the second equation by 5 and adding both we get:
\[\begin{gathered}
  IO_3^ - + 5{e^ - } + 6{H^ + } \to {I_2} + 3{H_2}O \\
  5{I^ - } \to 5{I_2} + 5{e^ - } \\
  \overline {IO_3^ - + 5{I^ - } + 6{H^ + } \to 3I_2^ - + 3{H_2}O\,} \\
\end{gathered} \]
The final equation is:
\[IO_3^ - + 5{I^ - } + 6{H^ + } \to 3{H_2}O + 3I_2^ - \]
\[a = 5;b = 6;c = 3;d = 3\]

Question: A thin circular wire carrying a current I has a magnetic moment M. The shape of the wire is changed to a square and it carries the same current. It will have a magnetic moment \[\frac{{\pi M}}{k}\], where k is:

$ = i \times \pi {\left( {\frac{L}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} = \frac{{i{L^2}}}{{4\pi }}$ ..... (i)
Finally for square coil $M' = i \times {\left( {\frac{L}{4}} \right)^2} = \frac{{i{L^2}}}{{16}}$ ..... (ii)

Solving equation (i) and (ii) $M' = \frac{{\pi M}}{4}$

Question: The main scale of a Vernier callipers is calibrated in mm and 19 divisions of main scale are equal in length to 20 divisions of Vernier scale. In measuring the diameter of a cylinder by this instrument, the main scale reads 35 divisions and 4th division of Vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. The radius of the cylinder is $$1.76 \times {10^{ - x}}m.$$ Find the value of x.
Solution:
20 Vernier scale division coincide with 19 mm scale divisions.

1 Vernier scale division will coincide with $$\frac{{19}}{{20}}\,mm$$ main scale divisions.

Thus, LC of vernier scale is $$1 - \frac{{19}}{{20}}\,mm = 0.05\,mm = 0.005\,cm$$

Main scale reading = 35 mm = 3.5 cm

Vernier scale reading = 4

Vernier scale reading $$ \times LC = 4 \times 0.05 = 0.2\,mm = 0.02\,cm$$

Total reading $$ = 3.5 + 0.02 = 3.52\,cm$$

Radius of cylinder $$ = \frac{{3.52}}{2} = 1.76\,cm = 1.76 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = 2$$

Question: If the angle between two vectors \[\hat i + \hat k\;\,{\text{and}}\,\;\hat i - \hat j + a\hat k\] is $\frac{\pi }{3}$, then the non-negative value of a is
Solution:
\[1 + a = \sqrt 2 \sqrt {2 + {a^2}} \times \frac{1}{2}\] \[\left( {{\text{Since}}\,\,\overrightarrow a .\overrightarrow b = \left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|\cos \theta } \right)\]
\[8a + 4 + 4{a^2} = 4 + 2{a^2}\]
\[8a + 2{a^2} = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;2a\left( {a + 4} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;a = 0,\; - 4\]

Question: Let A be the centre of the circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> – 2x – 4y – 20 = 0, and B (1,7) and D (4, –2) are points on the circle then, if tangents be drawn at B and D, which meet at C, then area of quadrilateral ABCD is-
Solution:
\[A \equiv (1,2),r = 5\]
Equation of tangent at B
\[x + 7y - (x + 1) - 2(y + 7) - 20 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 5y - 35 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow y = 7\,\,\,...(i)\]
Equation of tangent at D
\[4x - 2y - (x + 4) - 2(y - 2) - 20 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3x - 4y - 20 = 0\,\,\,...(ii)\]
From (i) and (ii)
\[C \equiv (16,7)\]
∴ Length of tangent
\[BC = \sqrt {256 + 49 - 32 - 28 - 20} = 15\]
\[\therefore area\,ABCD = 2 \times areaABC\]
\[ = 2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 5 \times 15 = 75\]

Question: The number of terms of the G.P. \[3,\frac{3}{2},\frac{3}{4},...\] needed to obtain a sum of \[\frac{{3069}}{{512}}\] is
Solution:
Here \[a = 3,r = \frac{1}{2}\]
Let the number of terms needed be $n,$ then
\[\begin{array}{l}{S_n} = \frac{{a\left( {1 - {r^n}} \right)}}{{1 - r}}\\\frac{{3069}}{{512}} = \frac{{3\left( {1 - {{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)}^n}} \right)}}{{\left( {1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right)}}\\ \Rightarrow \,\,\frac{{1023}}{{1024}} = 1 - {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n}\\ \Rightarrow \,\,{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^n} = \frac{1}{{1024}}\,\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,n = 10\end{array}\]

Question: If $x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^2} + y - 2\,,\,\,y\left( 1 \right) = 1,$ then $y\left( 2 \right)$ equals _____.
Solution:
Given $x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^2} + y - 2$ or $\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \frac{1}{x}y = \left( {x - \frac{2}{x}} \right)$
I.F. $ = {e^{ - \int {\frac{1}{x}dx} }} = {e^{ - \ln x}} = \frac{1}{x}$
Now, general solution is given by
$\frac{y}{x} = \int {\left( {x - \frac{2}{x}} \right)\frac{1}{x}dx} $ or $\frac{y}{x} = x + \frac{2}{x} + {\text{C}}$
As $y\left( 1 \right) = 1\,,\,\,{\text{C}} = - 2$
$\therefore \,\,\,\frac{y}{x} = x + \frac{2}{x} - 2$ or $y = {x^2} - 2x + 2$
Hence, $y\left( 2 \right) = {\left( 2 \right)^2} - 2\left( 2 \right) + 2 = 2$

Question: The work functions of Silver and Sodium are 4.6 eV and 2.3 eV, respectively. The ratio of the slope of the stopping potential versus frequency plot for Silver to that of Sodium is
Solution:
Slope = \[\frac{h}{e}\]does not depends on \[\phi \]
Ratio = 1

We have the relation \[h{v}= h{v_0} + KE\]

\[ = h{v_0} + e{V_0}\,\,\,\,\,\] [\[{V_0} = \] stopping potential]

So, \[{V_0} = h/e\left( {v - {v_0}} \right)\]

Or, \[{V_0} = \left( {h/e} \right)v - \left( {h/e} \right){v_0}\]

This is of the form \[y = mx + c\]

Slope \[ = m = h/e\] and y-intercept \[ =  - h{v_0}/e\]

Question: The IE<sub>1</sub>, IE<sub>2</sub>, IE<sub>3</sub>, IE<sub>4</sub> and IE<sub>5</sub> of an element are 7.1, 14.3, 32.5, 46.8 and 162.2 eV respectively. The stable oxidation state of the element is_____.

Question: If \[\int {\frac{{{{\sec }^3}x}}{{\sqrt {\sin 2x} }}dx} = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{\lambda }\left( {{{\tan }^2}x + \lambda } \right)\sqrt {\tan x} + C,\] where $C$ is a constant of integration, then $\lambda = \,$_____.
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{I}} = \int {\frac{{{{\sec }^3}x}}{{\sqrt {\sin 2x} }}dx} \\
  \,\,\, = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\int {\frac{{{{\sec }^3}x\,dx}}{{\sqrt {\sin x\cos x} }}} \\
  \,\,\, = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\int {\frac{{{{\sec }^4}x\,dx}}{{\sqrt {\tan x} }}} \\
  {\text{Putting}}\,\,\,t = \tan x\,\,,\,\,\,{\text{we}}\,\,{\text{get}}\,\,\,dt = {\sec ^2}x\,dx \\
  \therefore \,\,\,{\text{I}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\int {\frac{{1 + {t^2}}}{{\sqrt t }}dt} \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\int {\left( {{t^{ - \,\frac{1}{2}}} + {t^{\frac{3}{2}}}} \right)dt} \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\left( {2{t^{\frac{1}{2}}} + \frac{2}{5}{t^{\frac{5}{2}}}} \right) + C \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{5}\left( {5\sqrt {\tan x} + {{\tan }^2}x\sqrt {\tan x} } \right) + C \\
  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{5}\left( {{{\tan }^2}x + 5} \right)\sqrt {\tan x} + C \\
  \therefore \,\,\,\lambda = 5 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The outcome of each of 30 items was observed; 10 items gave an outcome $\frac{1}{2} - d$ each, 10 items gave outcome $\frac{1}{2}$each and the remaining 10 items gave outcome $\frac{1}{2} + d$ each. If the variance of this outcome data is $\frac{4}{3}$and |d| equals $\sqrt k $, then k is equal to:
Solution:
Outcomes are $\left( {\frac{1}{2} - d} \right),\left( {\frac{1}{2} - d} \right),....,$ 10 times $\frac{1}{2},\frac{1}{2},....,$ 10 times, $\frac{1}{2} + d,\frac{1}{2} + d,...$ 10 times
Mean = $\frac{1}{{30}}\left( {\frac{1}{2} \times 30} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$
Variance of the outcomes is,
$\begin{gathered}
  {\sigma ^2} = \frac{1}{{30}}\sum {x_i^2 - {{\left( {\bar x} \right)}^2}} \\
   = \frac{1}{{30}}\left[ {{{\left( {\frac{1}{2} - d} \right)}^2} \times 10 + {{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)}^2} \times 10 + {{\left( {\frac{1}{2} + d} \right)}^2} \times 10} \right] - \frac{1}{4} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} = \frac{1}{{30}}\left[ {30 \times \frac{1}{4} + 20{d^2}} \right] - \frac{1}{4} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{3}{d^2} - \frac{1}{4} \\
   \Rightarrow {d^2} = 2 \Rightarrow \left| d \right| = \sqrt 2 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The two wires shown in figure are made of same material which has a breaking stress of $8 \times {10^5}N/{m^2}.$ The area of cross section of the upper wire is 0.006 $c{m^2}.$ The mass ${m_1} = 10kg$ and ${m_2} = 20\,kg.$Hanger is light. The maximum weight that can be put on the hanger without breaking any of the wire is $\frac{{70}}{n}kg.$ Find value of $n.$
Solution:
Lower wire will break if
$\left( {8 \times {{10}^8}} \right) = \,\frac{{\left( {10 + m} \right) \times 10}}{{0.003 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}\therefore \,\,m = 14\,kg$
Upper wire will break if
$\left( {8 \times {{10}^8}} \right) = \,\frac{{\left( {10 + 20 + m} \right) \times 10}}{{0.006 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}\,\,\therefore m = 18\,kg$
$\therefore $ Answer is 14 kg.

Question: Two wires are made of the same material and have the same volume. However, wire 1 has cross-sectional area A and wire 2 has cross-sectional area 3A. If the length of wire 1 increases by $\Delta x$ on applying force F, the  force needed to stretch wire 2 by the same amount is nF, where n is:
Solution:
Let ${L_1}$ and ${L_2}$ be the lengths of wires 1 and 2.
Given ${V_1} = {V_2} \Rightarrow A{L_1} = 3A{L_2} \Rightarrow \frac{{{L_1}}}{{{L_2}}} = 3.$
If F and $F'$ are the forces applied to 1 and 2.
$Y = \frac{{F{L_1}}}{{A\Delta x}} = \frac{{F'{L_2}}}{{3A\Delta x}} \Rightarrow \frac{{F'}}{F} = \frac{{3{L_1}}}{{{L_2}}} = 3 \times 3 = 9.$
Thus $F' = 9F.$

Question: The minimum horizontal acceleration of the container so that the pressure at the point A becomes atmospheric is \[\frac{n}{3}g\], where n is: (tank has sufficient height).
Solution:
Volume equality gives

$$2 \times 3 = \frac{1}{2} \times h \times 3 \Rightarrow h = 4\;{\text{m}}$$

$$\therefore \tan \theta  = \frac{4}{3} = \frac{a}{g} \Rightarrow a = \frac{4}{3}g$$

Question: When a circular coil of radius 1 m and 100 turns is rotated in a horizontal uniform magnetic field, the peak value of emf induced is 100 V. The coil is unwound and then rewound into a circular coil of radius 2 m. If it is rotated, now, with the same speed under similar conditions, the new peak value of emf (in V) developed is
Solution:
Initial no. of coils N = 100 and radius = 1 m
\[2\pi f \times 100 \times B \times \pi \times {1^2} = 100V\]
Total length = 100 \[ \times \] \[2\pi \] \[ \times \] 1 = 200\[\pi \]m
The circumference of turn of radius 2m = 2\[\pi \] \[ \times \] 2m
No.of turns in 2\[\pi \] \[ \times \] 100 m is \[\frac{{2\pi \times 100}}{{2\pi \times 2}} = 50\] turns
\[V_m^1 = 2\pi \times f \times 50 \times B \times \pi \times {2^2} = 200V\]

Question: An ideal massless spring $S$ can be compressed 1 m by a force of 100 N in equilibrium. The same spring is placed at the bottom of a frictionless plane inclined at 30o to the horizontal. A 10 kg block $M$ is released from rest at the top of the incline and is brought to rest momentarily after compressing the spring by 2 m. if g = 10 m/s2, the speed of mass just before it touches the spring is $\sqrt {10x} \,{\text{m/s}}$. Find value of x?
Solution:
$F = kx$
$\therefore \,\,k = \frac{F}{x}$
$ = \,\frac{{100}}{1}{\text{N/m}}$
Now from energy conservation, between natural length of spring and its maximum compression state.
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + mgh = \frac{1}{2}k{x^2}_{\max }$
\[\therefore \,\,v = \sqrt {\frac{{k{x^2}_{\max }}}{m} - 2gh} \]
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{\left( {100} \right){{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}}{{10}} - \left( 2 \right)\left( {10} \right)\left( {\frac{2}{2}} \right)} $
$ = \,\sqrt {20} \,{\text{m/s}}$

Question: The least integral value of $x$ where $f\left( x \right) = {\log _{1/2}}\left( {{x^2} - 2x - 3} \right)$ is monotonically decreasing is _____.
Solution:
${x^2} - 2x - 3 > 0$
or $\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right) > 0$
i.e., $x < - 1\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,x > 3$      … (1)
Now, $f\left( x \right) = {\log _{1/2}}\left( {{x^2} - 2x - 3} \right)$
$ = \frac{{{{\log }_e}\left( {{x^2} - 2x - 3} \right)}}{{{{\log }_e}\left( {1/2} \right)}}$
$f'\left( x \right) = \frac{{2x - 2}}{{\left( {{{\log }_e}\left( {1/2} \right)} \right)\left( {{x^2} - 2x - 3} \right)}}$
For $f\left( x \right)$ to be decreasing, $f'\left( x \right) < 0$
or $\frac{{x - 1}}{{\left( {{{\log }_e}\left( {1/2} \right)} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} < 0$
or $x > 1$      … (2)
From (1) and (2), $x > 3$

Hence, required least integral value of $x$ is $4.$

Question: The reaction X → B is a zeroth order reaction. If the initial concentration of X is 0.2M, the half-life is 6 h. When the initial concentration of X is 0.4 M, the time required to reach its final concentration of 0.2 M in hours will be
Solution:
For the reaction X → B, follows zeroth order. Rate equation is
Kt = [A]0 – [A]
For the half-life; t = t1/2and [A] = 0.1
Kt1/2 = 0.2 – 0.1
\[\frac{{0.2 - 0.1}}{6} = \frac{{0.1}}{6}Mh{r^{ - 1}}\]
Time required to reach from 0.4 M to 0.2 M
Kt = [A]0 – [A]
\[\frac{{0.1}}{6} \times t = (0.4 - 0.2);\;t = 12hour\]

Question:

P.D. across 800 Ω resistors = 5.6 V
So, ${I_{800\Omega }} = \frac{{5.6}}{{800}}A = 7{\text{mA}}$
Now, PD. across 200 Ω resistors = $\left( {9 - 5.6} \right)V = 3.4V$
So, ${I_{200\Omega }} = \frac{{9 - 5.6}}{{200}} = 17mA$
so, current through zener diode
$ = {I_z} = 17 - 7 = 10{\text{ mA}}$

Question: The mass of an oxygen molecule is about 16 times that of a hydrogen molecule. At room temperature the rms speed of oxygen molecules is $\upsilon .$The rms speed of the hydrogen molecules at the same temperature will be $n\,\upsilon $,where n is
Solution:
\[v = \sqrt {\frac{{3kT}}{m}} .\,{\text{Therefore, }}{\upsilon _O} = \sqrt {\frac{{3kT}}{{{m_O}}}} \,{\text{and}}\]
${\upsilon _H} = \sqrt {\frac{{3kT}}{{{m_H}}}} $.
Thus $\frac{{{\upsilon _O}}}{{{\upsilon _H}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_H}}}{{{m_O}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{1}{{16}}} = \frac{1}{4}$
or ${\upsilon _H} = 4{\upsilon _O}$

Question:
In the reaction C(s) + CO2(g) → 2CO(g), the equilibrium pressure is 12 atm.
If 50% of CO2 reacts at equilibrium, the initial pressure is (in atm) _______.
Solution:
Total number of moles at equilibrium = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5 moles
\[{P_{C{O_2}}} = \frac{{0.5 \times 12}}{{1.5}} = 4\,\,atm\]

∴ Initial pressure = 8 atm \[(\because \,\,At\,\,equi.,\,\,0.5\,mol\,\,C{O_2}\,\,has\,\,pressure = 4\,\,atm\,\,;1\,\,mol\,\,C{O_2}\,\,has\,\, = 8\,\,atm\,\,pressure)\]

Question: Bond dissociation energy of XY, X<sub>2</sub> and Y<sub>2</sub> (all diatomic molecules) are in the ratio of 1 : 1 : 0.5 and ∆H<sub>f</sub> of XY is  −100 kJ mol<sup>−</sup><sup>1</sup>. The bond dissociation energy of X<sub>2</sub> is 100 x. Find the value of x
Solution:
\[\frac{1}{2}{X_2} + \frac{1}{2}{Y_2}\, \to \,\,XY\,\,;\,\,\,\Delta H = - 100\,\,kJ/mol\]
Bond dissociation energy ratio 1 : 0.5 : 1
a : \[\frac{a}{2}\,:\,\,a\]
\[\left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\Delta {H_{r \times n}} = \sum BE(R) - \sum BE(P)} \\
  { - 100 = \frac{a}{2} + \frac{a}{4} - a\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,}
\end{array}} \right.\]
∴ \[\frac{a}{2} + \frac{a}{4} - a = - 100\]
a = 400
Thus, 100 x = 400
x = 4

Question: The plates of parallel plate capacitor are charged upto$100\;V$. A $2mm$ thick plate is inserted between the plates. Then to maintain the same potential difference, the distance between the plates is increased by$1.6\;mm$. The dielectric constant of the plate is
Solution:
In air the potential difference between the plates
${V_{air}} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}.d$ ..... (i)
In the presence of partially filled medium potential difference between the plates
${V_m} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}(d - t + \frac{t}{K})$ ..... (ii)
Potential difference between the plates with dielectric medium and increased distance is
${V_m}' = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}\left\{ {(d + d') - t + \frac{t}{K}} \right\}$ ..... (iii)
According to question ${V_{air}} = {V_m}'$ which gives $K = \frac{t}{{t - d'}}$
Hence $K = \frac{2}{{2 - 1.6}} = 5$

Question: An infinitely long conductor PQR is bent to form a right angle as shown. A current I flows through PQR. The magnetic field due to this current at the point M is H1. Now another infinitely long straight conductor QS is connected at Q so that the current is I/2 in QR as well as in QS, The current in PQ remains unchanged. The magnetic field at M is now ${H_{2.}}$The ratio ${H_2}/{H_1}$ is given by $$\frac{x}{2}$$, then $$x$$ is:
Solution:
Magnetic field at any point lying on the current carrying straight conductor is zero.
Here H1 = Magnetic field at M due to current in PQ.
H2 = Magnetic field at M due to QR
+ magnetic field at M due to QS
        + magnetic field at M due to PQ
     $ = 0 + \frac{{{H_1}}}{2} + {H_1} = \frac{3}{2}{H_1}$ ⇒ $\frac{{{H_2}}}{{{H_1}}} = \frac{3}{2}$

As $$\frac{x}{2} = \frac{3}{2},$$

$$\therefore x = 3$$

Question: A uniform rod of mass m, length\[\ell \]rotates about its end point O in horizontal plane. If the rod is rotating with a constant angular speed on a frictionless surface and the ratio of restoring force developed in the rod at points A and C is $\frac{{{F_A}}}{{{F_C}}} = 1 + \frac{n}{{27}}$ where ‘n’ is an integer value, find n.
n AM’s are inserted between 2 and 38. If third AM is 14 then n is equal to
Solution:

\[\therefore 14 = 2 + 3d \Rightarrow d = 4\]

Question: For given spring mass system, If the time period of small oscillations of block about its mean position is $\pi \sqrt {\frac{{nm}}{K}} ,$ then find n. Assume ideal conditions. The system is in vertical plane and take K1 = 2K, K2 = K.
Solution:
In equilibrium position elongations in springs are related as $$\frac{{{k_1}{x_{10}}}}{2} = {k_2}{x_{20}} = mg$$ After displacement of block by x downwards,  If block is displaced by x downwards, x1 and x2 are extra elongations in springs k1 and k2 respectively
$F = mg - {k_2}\left( {\frac{{mg}}{{{k_2}}} + {x_2}} \right) = - {k_2}{x_2}....(1)$
and ${k_1}\left( {\frac{{2mg}}{{{k_1}}} + {x_1}} \right) = 2{k_2}\left( {\frac{{mg}}{{{k_2}}} + {x_2}} \right) \Rightarrow {k_1}{x_1} = 2{k_2}{x_2} \Rightarrow {x_1} = {x_2}.....(2)$
Constraint relation is
x = x2 + 2x1 = 3x2. . . . (3)
From (1), (2) and (3),
$F = - \frac{k}{3}x,$ So, $T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{m}{{\frac{k}{3}}}} = \pi \sqrt {\frac{{12m}}{k}} $

ALTERNATE 

$$\frac{1}{{{K_{eq}}}} = \frac{{{1^2}}}{{{K_2}}} + \frac{{{2^2}}}{{{K_1}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{{{K_{eq}}}} = \frac{1}{K} + \frac{4}{{2K}} = \frac{3}{K}$$

$${K_{eq}} = \frac{K}{3} \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{3m}}{K}} $$

Question: The displacement $y$ (in meter) of a body varies with time $t$ (in second) as $y =  - \frac{2}{3}{t^2} + 16t + 2$. The body comes to rest in a time (in s).
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  y =  - \frac{2}{3}{t^2} + 16t + 2   \\
  v = \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} =  - \frac{2}{3} \times 2t + 16   \\
  v = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}t = 16 \Rightarrow t = 12s   \\ 
\end{gathered} \]

Question: A capacitor \[C = 10\mu F\] is connected across a battery of emf 10V. After it is charged to 10V, the battery is disconnected and reconnected across the capacitor with reversed polarity. The heat (in m J) dissipated in the circuit on reconnection is
Solution:
\[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\] = change in energy stored in capacitor $$ + $$ heat dissipated
Since Change in energy stored in capacitor is zero as C and V both don't change in value.
\[\therefore \] heat dissipated = \[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\]
\[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\] $$ = 2qV$$ because Initially if charge $$ + q$$ charge was on the plate then due to reverse polarity of battery, $$ - q$$ charge will appear on that plate which means total $$2q$$ charge was given by the battery. 

Now using $$q = CV$$ we get 

$${W_{{\text{Battery}}}} = 2C{V^2} = $$ Heat dissipated

Question: There is a hollow spherical planet of inner radius \[{R_1}\]and outer radius \[{R_2}\]and uniform mass density \[\rho \]. If the frequency of revolution of a satellite revolving around the planet in an orbit of radius 5R is given by \[f = {\left[ {\left( {1 + \frac{1}{{{N^2}}}} \right)\frac{{G\rho }}{{15\pi }}} \right]^{  1/2}}\]. Find the value of N. \[(Given:\,{R_1} = R,{R_2} = 3R)\]
Solution:
\[{F_g} = m{E_g} = \frac{{mv_0^2}}{{5R}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{{v_0^2}}{{5R}} = \frac{G}{{{{(5R)}^2}}}\left[ {\rho \frac{4}{3}\pi {{(3R)}^3} - \rho \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}} \right]\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,{v_0} = {\left[ {\frac{{4\pi G\rho {R^2}}}{3}\left( {\frac{{26}}{5}} \right)} \right]^{1/2}}\]
\[f = \frac{{{V_0}}}{{2\pi 5R}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi 5R}}{\left( {\frac{{4\pi G\rho R \times 26}}{{3 \times 5}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}\]
\[ = {\left( {\frac{{G\rho }}{{15\pi }}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt {26} }}{{\sqrt {25} }}} \right)\]
\[ = {\left[ {\left( {1 + \frac{1}{{{5^2}}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{G\rho }}{{15\pi }}} \right)} \right]^{\frac{1}{2}}}\]

Question: Capacitor $A$ has a capacitance of $15\mu F$ when it is filled with a medium of dielectric constant 15. Another capacitor $B$ has a capacitance of $1\mu F$with air between the plates. Both are charged separately by a battery of$100\;V$. After charging, both are connected in parallel without the battery and the dielectric medium being removed. The common potential now is 100 m volts where m is:
Solution:
Charge on capacitor A is given by ${Q_1} = 15 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 100 = 15 \times {10^{ - 4}}C$
Charge on capacitor B is given by ${Q_2} = 1 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 100 = {10^{ - 4}}C$
Capacity of capacitor A after removing dielectric $ = \frac{{15 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{15}} = 1\mu F$
Now when both capacitors are connected in parallel their equivalent capacitance will be Ceq $ = 1 + 1 = 2\mu F$
So common potential $ = \frac{{(15 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}) + (1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}})}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 800V.$
